title: A General Quantum Speed Limit for Non-Hermitian Systems
authors: Zhanxi Wang, Xiaozhe Hao, X. X. Yi
abstract: The quantum speed limit (QSL) refers to the maximum speed of a quantum system to evolve from an initial state to its orthogonal states. The bound on the QSL for Hermitian systems, for example the Mandelstam-Tamm (MT) and Margolus-Levitin (ML) as well as Sun-Zheng(SZ) bound, was studied respectively from the perspectives of average value and variance of the system Hamiltonian as well as the geometry of the system. While the compactness of the MT-type, ML-type and SZ-type bounds has been examined well for Hermitian systems, a compact QSL for non-Hermitian systems has not been well studied. In this work, based on the biorthogonal basis theory we derive two distinct and tighter bounds on the QSL for non-Hermitian systems, which correspond to the MT and ML bounds for Hermitian systems. We show that the shortest evolution time corresponding to the two bounds of the non-Hermitian system can be attained by certain initial states, showing the compactness and tightness of our bounds. These initial states dubbed fastest initial states(FIS) are different from that in Hermitian systems. A bound close to QSL for non-FIS is presented and comparison of our bound with others in literature is performed. To illustrate our results, we present a minimal non-Hermitian system to show QSL, and the condition for the shortest evolution time is derived analytically using the present theory.
summary: This paper establishes new fundamental limits on how fast a non-Hermitian quantum system can evolve to an orthogonal state. By extending the well-known MT and ML bounds to non-Hermitian dynamics, the authors identify the specific initial states that allow for the fastest possible evolution.
main_problem: The paper aims to derive and establish tight, compact Quantum Speed Limit (QSL) bounds, specifically of the Mandelstam-Tamm (MT) and Margolus-Levitin (ML) types, for non-Hermitian quantum systems.
main_result: The authors derive two new, tighter bounds for non-Hermitian QSL that correspond to the MT and ML bounds in Hermitian systems and identify 'fastest initial states' (FIS) that achieve the minimum evolution time.
method: The derivation utilizes biorthogonal basis theory and trigonometric inequalities applied to the real and imaginary parts of the state overlap within a non-degenerate non-Hermitian framework.
model_or_system: A general non-degenerate non-Hermitian Hamiltonian with complex eigenvalues, including specific applications to a two-level system and a three-level PT-symmetric wireless power transfer (WPT) system.
key_observables: Quantum Speed Limit (tau), ML-type bound (F_ML), MT-type bound (F_MT), and Geometric-type bound (F_G).
important_parameters: Real and imaginary parts of eigenvalues (omega and gamma), the ratio of bounds (alpha), and the parameter delta for studying the approach to the shortest evolution time.
main_assumptions: The system is assumed to be non-degenerate and the Hamiltonian is time-independent; a shifted Hamiltonian is used to ensure non-negative eigenvalues.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares the difference between the combined QSL bound and the geometric bound against parameters theta and alpha, identifying regions of existence for solutions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the QSL problem, develops the biorthogonal mathematical framework, derives the new MT and ML-type bounds, analyzes the tightness and saturation conditions of these bounds, and provides numerical illustrations using PT-symmetric systems.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and quantum optics, as it provides fundamental limits on the speed of state evolution in dissipative or gain-driven environments, which are central to non-Hermitian physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Left panel: ∆τ ′ = τ ′ comb −τ ′ G versus θ and α. Region A (blue) is for τ ′ comb < τ ′ G, region B (orange) is for τ ′ comb > τ ′ G, and region C (purple) is for τ ′ comb and τ ′ G having no solution. Right panel: ∆τ ′ with θ ∈[0.485π, 0.505π] and α ∈[0.4π, 0.45π]. The red region is plotted for reference, where ∆τ ′ = 0.

FIG. 2: (a), ∆τ versus α/π for different µ/ν. Green solid line is for µ/ν = 0.5, red dotted line µ/ν = 1, and blue dashed dot line µ/ν = 1.5. (b) The same as in Figure (a) but with −µ/ν with respect to that in Figure (a).

FIG. 3: FML(τ) (a) and FMT(τ) (b) versus time τ. τ is the time for the quantum system to evolve from an initial state to its orthogonal ones. To plot this figure, we choose κ = 2.5 and η, ς = 1. The red points are for random initial states, the blue line corresponds to the shortest evolution time τmin = π

FIG. 4: Upper and lower bound of the evolution time needed for the system from an initial states to its orthogonal states as a function of κ/η (ς = 1). The red points in the figure are obtained for randomly generated initial states. The blue solid line bounds the shortest evo- lution time τmin.
